findings.title: yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. Pin the reference to a full 40-character commit SHA instead, e.g. `uses: actions/checkout@8ade135a41bc03ea155e62e844d188df1ea18608`.
findings.description: Semgrep rule yaml.github-actions.security.github-actions-mutable-action-tag.github-actions-mutable-action-tag detected: GitHub Actions step uses a mutable tag or branch reference. Tags and branch names can be silently repointed by the action owner, enabling supply-chain attacks — as seen in the trivy-action and kics-github-action compromises. findings.code_snippet: 12 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can compromise the GitHub Actions marketplace or the repository of the action owner (e.g., actions/checkout) and repoint the tag to a malicious commit.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker compromises the repository hosting the action (e.g., actions/checkout) and pushes a malicious commit to the tag referenced (e.g., v4). 2. The next time the workflow runs (line 12 of Build, Test, Lint & Format.yml), GitHub Actions fetches the malicious code. 3. The malicious code executes with the permissions of the workflow, potentially exfiltrating secrets or modifying the repository.
attack_scenario.impact: The attacker gains arbitrary code execution within the CI/CD pipeline, allowing exfiltration of repository secrets (e.g., AWS keys, npm tokens) or modification of build artifacts.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding correctly identifies a mutable tag reference. The attack path is well-documented in real-world supply-chain attacks. The precondition (compromised action owner) is realistic for popular actions.

If an attacker can control a process running as root, they may have control over the container. Ensure that the last USER in a Dockerfile is a USER other than 'root'.
findings.code_snippet: 14 | requires login

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can exploit a vulnerability in the application running inside the container (e.g., RCE via a web endpoint) to gain code execution as root.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker exploits a vulnerability in the Flask app (e.g., command injection, SSTI) to execute arbitrary commands. 2. Because the container runs as root (no USER directive in Dockerfile line 14), the attacker's commands run with root privileges. 3. The attacker can then escape the container (e.g., by mounting host filesystem) or compromise other containers on the same host.
attack_scenario.impact: The attacker gains root-level access inside the container, enabling container escape and potential host compromise.
attack_scenario.explanation: The Dockerfile does not specify a USER, so the process runs as root. This is a real security risk, though the severity depends on the application's attack surface. The finding is valid.

findings.description:
Semgrep rule python.django.security.audit.unvalidated-password.unvalidated-password detected: The password on 'user' is being set without validating the password. Call django.contrib.auth.password_validation.validate_password() with validation functions before setting the password. See https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.0/topics/auth/passwords/ for more information.
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — This is a Django-specific rule applied to a Flask application. The code in auth.py uses Flask-SQLAlchemy and Werkzeug's generate_password_hash, not Django's authentication system. The password is hashed before storage, so the 'unvalidated password' concern is not applicable. The finding is a false positive due to framework mismatch.]
findings.code_snippet: 68 | requires login

attack_scenario.explanation: This is a Django-specific rule applied to a Flask application. The code in auth.py uses Flask-SQLAlchemy and Werkzeug's generate_password_hash, not Django's authentication system. The password is hashed before storage, so the 'unvalidated password' concern is not applicable. The finding is a false positive due to framework mismatch.

findings.title: Potential Encryption/secret key hardcoded
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Encryption/secret key hardcoded at config.py:17. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.
findings.code_snippet: 17 | SECRET_KEY = "test-secret-key"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker who can access the source code (e.g., via public repository, insider, or leaked file). No authentication required.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., clones public repo or reads leaked file). 2. Attacker reads line 17 of config.py to extract the SECRET_KEY = "test-secret-key". 3. Attacker uses this key to forge Flask session cookies, decrypt session data, or sign arbitrary data.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can forge session cookies, impersonate any user, or decrypt session contents. This can lead to full account takeover and privilege escalation.
attack_scenario.explanation: The SECRET_KEY is hardcoded with a weak test value. If this code is deployed, an attacker with access to the source can use the key to compromise session integrity. The key is not a placeholder for a real secret; it is a literal test string.

findings.code_snippet: 18 | JWT_SECRET_KEY = "test-jwt-secret-key"

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker who can access the source code (e.g., via public repository, insider, or leaked file). No authentication required.
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code. 2. Attacker reads line 18 of config.py to extract JWT_SECRET_KEY = "test-jwt-secret-key". 3. Attacker uses this key to forge or modify JWT tokens, bypassing authentication or authorization.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can forge valid JWT tokens for any user, including admins, leading to full account takeover and unauthorized access to protected resources.
attack_scenario.explanation: The JWT_SECRET_KEY is hardcoded with a weak test value. If the application uses JWT for authentication, an attacker with the source can forge tokens. This is a real vulnerability.

Dockerfile uses COPY . which copies everything including .git, .env, and sensitive files into the image.
Found at line 8: COPY . .

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Action in Build, Test, Lint & Format.yml
